title: Lesson 5 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Hey Luis 👋. Big lesson today — but stick with me. By the end you'll be ordering fruit at a Maltese market and bossing people around in Maltese. Three minutes. Let's go.
Two ideas. One — Maltese has a funny habit with fruit and veg. Two — there's a little magic trick that turns Maltese verbs on. Both easier than they sound.
OK, picture this. 🍎 You walk into a supermarket in Valletta. You grab one apple. Single apple in your hand:
Now zoom out. The whole apple aisle. Apples as a thing in the world — like when we say 'I love apples':
Just drop the 'a' off the end. That's apples in general.
Now your basket has six apples. Six specific apples:
An '-iet' on the end means a specific few.
So that's three flavours of the same word. ONE. IN GENERAL. SPECIFIC FEW.
Sounds weird, right? But here's the good news. It works the same for almost every fruit and vegetable. Once you've got the pattern, you've got the lot.
Quick taste. 🍇 Grapes:
one grape.
grapes in general.
specific grapes. Same shape every time.
Same for oranges. 🍊
One. The thing in general. Specific many.

When does this matter in real life? At the shop. 'I want an orange' is singular. 'I love oranges' is collective. 'Three oranges, please' is plural. Catch yourself slipping into Maltese mode at your next grocery run. 😄
Same trick for veg. 🥬 Onion:
Tomato:
Don't try to memorise all sixteen fruit and sixteen veg today. Open the cards, tap one, listen, tap another. Your brain catches the pattern way faster than you'd expect.
OK. Take a breath. ✋ Half the lesson done.
Now verbs. Forget the word 'imperative' for a second — let's call it the boss voice.
The boss voice is what you use when you tell someone to do something. 'Sit down!' 'Walk!' 'Read this!' Short, sharp, telling somebody what to do.
In Maltese the boss voice has two flavours. One for telling ONE person. One for telling a GROUP.
Tell one friend to walk:
Tell a group of friends to walk:
One person, write:
A group, write:
Spot the trick? Stick a 'u' on the end and a one-person command becomes a group command. Done.
Now the magic. 🪄 Here's why the boss voice matters.
Once you can shout a command at someone in Maltese, you've basically already learned how to say 'I do it', 'you do it', 'he does it', 'she does it'. All from the same word.
Watch. The command 'go out!' is:
Stick an 'n' on the front — that's 'I'. 'I go out' becomes:
Stick a 't' on the front — that's 'you'. 'You go out' becomes:
Stick a 'j' on the front — that's 'he'. 'He goes out':
And 't' again for 'she'. Same as 'you'. They share that one. 'She goes out':
So the rhythm is N — T — J — T.
I — you — he — she. N — T — J — T. 🎵 Say it a few times. It's a beat. Once it's in your head, you can conjugate basically any Maltese verb.
And for 'we', 'you all', 'they'? Same letters again. N for we. T for you all. J for they. The plural prefixes match the singular ones.
So the whole pattern is N — T — J — T — N — T — J. Seven prefixes, only three letters. That's the entire Maltese present tense in a beat.

There are tiny tweaks when a verb starts with a consonant instead of a vowel — like 'sit', poġġi. The prefix gets a soft 'i' or 'n' to keep it pronounceable. Don't sweat it. The cards walk you through, and your ears will pick it up faster than your brain. 👂
That's the whole lesson. Fruit shapes, boss voice, magic letters. Tap into any section and let the audio do the work. You're doing brilliantly, Luis. Saħħa. 💪
